assessment.title: IST356 Diagnostic Assessment
assessment.course: IST356: Programming Techniques for Data Analytics
assessment.description: Python programming diagnostic. Use the results from this diagnostic to identify your readiness for IST356. This is a 20-question fill-in-the-blank assessment; for each question, determine exactly what the code prints. Answers are simple digits or text strings entered without quotation marks.
assessment.delivery: Questions delivered one at a time in randomized order.
assessment.image_encoding: code_image_base64 is a PNG of the code snippet, embedded as a data URI so this JSON is fully self-contained.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
x = 2
y = x * 2
z = x + y
print(z)
questions.explanation: x=2, then y = 2*2 = 4, then z = x + y = 2 + 4 = 6. print(z) shows 6.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
x = 3
print(f"{x}")
questions.explanation: An f-string substitutes the value of x into the {x} placeholder, so the output is the value 3.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
x = "100"
y = "1"
z = x + y
print(z)
questions.explanation: x and y are strings, so + concatenates rather than adds. "100" + "1" = "1001".

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
x = 1
y = 3
z = (x + y) * 2
print(z)
questions.explanation: Parentheses first: (1 + 3) = 4, then 4 * 2 = 8.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: The test x > 4 is 4 > 4 which is False, so the else branch runs and y = 3.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: 7 < 2 is False and 7 < 4 is False, so the else branch runs once: y = 0 + 1 = 1. Only one branch executes in an if/elif/else chain.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: x < 10 is True, so we enter the inner if. y is 4: 4 < 4 is False and 4 > 4 is False, so the inner else runs and prints 3.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: x = -5 is not > 0, so y = "b". w = 5 is > 0, so q = z + y = "c" + "b" = "cb".

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
k = 4
j = 0
for i in range(k):
    j = j + 1
print(j)
questions.explanation: range(4) yields 0,1,2,3 (four iterations). j starts at 0 and is incremented once per iteration, so j = 4.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: x increments to 1, 2, 3. When x becomes 3 the test x > 2 is True and break exits the loop, so x = 3 is printed.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: The loop counts the letter 'i' in "milkmike". 'i' appears twice (m-i-l-k-m-i-k-e), so c = 2.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
def doit(a, b):
    c = a*b
    return c

x = doit(2, 1)
y = doit(3, 2)
print(y)
questions.explanation: doit returns a*b. y = doit(3, 2) = 3 * 2 = 6. (x is computed but never printed.)

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
def doit(x: str) -> int:
    c = 0
    for i in x:
        c = c + 1
    return c

y = doit("mikemilk")
print(y)
questions.explanation: doit counts the characters in the string. "mikemilk" has 8 letters, so it returns 8.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
def f1(a):
    return a + a

def f2(a):
    return a + 1

x = 5
y = f1(x)
z = f2(x)
print(z)
questions.explanation: z = f2(x) = f2(5) = 5 + 1 = 6. (y = f1(5) = 10 is computed but not printed.)

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
def f1(a):
    return a + a

def f2(a):
    return a + 1

x = 5
z = f2(f1(x))
print(z)
questions.explanation: Inner call first: f1(5) = 5 + 5 = 10. Then f2(10) = 10 + 1 = 11.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
text = "wordsmith"
s = text[5:]
print(s)
questions.explanation: Slicing text[5:] takes characters from index 5 to the end. "wordsmith" indices 0..8; from index 5 onward is "mith".

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.code:
t = "freefall"
s = t[:5]
print(s)
questions.explanation: Slicing t[:5] takes the first 5 characters (indices 0-4) of "freefall", which is "freef".

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: q takes each character of "bucket"; q[0] is just that character. None equal "i", so break never runs and the loop finishes with q holding the last character, "t".

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: doit returns 1 only for the letter 'i'. Summing over "missings" counts the 'i's: there are 2, so n = 2.

questions.prompt: Determine exactly what the following Python code prints when executed. Enter only the output (no quotation marks).
questions.explanation: doit is True when the first and last characters match. 'ada' (a==a) True, 'onko' (o==o) True, 'abba' (a==a) True, 'iris' (i==s) False. Three are True, so n = 3.
